elements.sent-2411-001: We report a comprehensive numerical study of the renormalization group flow of marginal couplings in $(3+1)$-dimensional projectable Ho\v rava gravity.
elements.sent-2411-002: First, we classify all fixed points of the flow and analyze their stability matrices.
elements.sent-2411-003: We find that some of the stability matrices possess complex eigenvalues and discuss why this does not contradict unitarity.
elements.sent-2411-004: Next, we scan over the renormalization group trajectories emanating from all asymptotically free fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-005: We identify a unique fixed point giving rise to a set of trajectories spanning the whole range of the kinetic coupling $\lambda$ compatible with unitarity.
elements.sent-2411-006: This includes the region $0<\lambda-1\ll 1$ assumed in previous phenomenological applications.
elements.sent-2411-007: The respective trajectories closely follow a single universal trajectory, differing only by the running of the gravitational coupling.
elements.sent-2411-008: The latter exhibits non-monotonic behavior along the flow, vanishing both in the ultraviolet and the infrared limits.
elements.sent-2411-009: The requirement that the theory remains weakly coupled along the renormalization group trajectory implies a natural hierarchy between the scale of Lorentz invariance violation and a much larger value of the Planck mass inferred from low-energy interactions.
elements.sent-2411-010: Ho\v rava gravity (HG)~\cite{Horava:2009uw (see \cite{Mukohyama:2010xz,Sotiriou:2010wn,Barvinsky:2023mrv,Herrero-Valea:2023zex for reviews), is an approach to building unitary, local and renormalizable theory of quantum gravity in four spacetime dimensions.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: It considers metric theories with an action classically invariant at high energies under anisotropic (Lifshitz) scaling of time and space, \be t\mapsto b^{-d t\;, \qquad x^i\mapsto b^{-1 x^i\;, \quad i=1,\dots,d \:, \ee where $d$ is the number of spatial dimensions and $b$ is an arbitrary scaling parameter.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: This allows one to include in the action terms with higher spatial derivatives which lead to a faster decay of propagators at high momenta and thereby improve convergence of the loop integrals.
elements.sent-2411-013: On the other hand, the theory remains quadratic in time derivatives and avoids Ostrogradsky instabilities arising in covariant higher-derivative gravity \cite{Stelle:1976gc,Stelle:1977ry .}
elements.sent-2411-014: For $d>1$ the scaling \eqref{scaling is incompatible with the full diffeomorphism invariances which needs to be restricted to foliation preserving transformations (FDiff): \be t\mapsto t'(t)\;, \qquad x^i\mapsto x'^i(t,{\bf x )\;, \ee where $t'(t)$ is a monotonic function.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A consequence of this property is explicit breaking of the Lorentz invariance which can emerge only as an approximate symmetry at low energies.
elements.sent-2411-016: While satisfying the stringent experimental constraints on deviations from Lorentz invariance \cite{Kostelecky:2008ts,Liberati:2013xla is challenging, there are several mechanisms that could achieve this goal \cite{GrootNibbelink:2004za,Pospelov:2010mp, Pujolas:2011sk,Anber:2011xf,Bednik:2013nxa,Kharuk:2015wga,Baggioli:2024vza .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: HG appears in two main versions differring by their field content.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the {\it non-projectable version \cite{Horava:2009uw,Blas:2009qj the lapse function (the time-time component of the metric) is taken to be fully dynamical, with both time and space dependence.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Whereas in the {\it projectable HG the lapse is restricted to depend only on time.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Low-energy phenomenology of the non-projectable HG in a certain region of parameter space can be sufficiently close to that of general relativity (GR) to pass the observational tests \cite{Blas:2014aca,EmirGumrukcuoglu:2017cfa .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, its complicated structure in the ultraviolet (UV) has so far precluded a rigorous proof of perturbative renormalizability, even though a remarkable cancellation of non-local divergences found in Ref.~\cite{Bellorin:2022np suggests that such proof is plausible.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper we focus on the simpler projectable model.
elements.sent-2411-023: This has been proven to be perturbatively renormalizable in any number of spacetime dimensions~\cite{Barvinsky:2015kil,Barvinsky:2017zlx and the one-loop beta-functions for its full set of marginal operators with respect to the scaling (\ref{scaling ) were computed in $d=2$ \cite{Barvinsky:2017kob and $d=3$ \cite{towards,3+1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In both cases the theory possesses asymptotically free UV fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-025: The global structure of the renormalization group (RG) flow for projectable HG in $d=2$ is rather simple and was completely characterized in Ref.~\cite{Barvinsky:2017kob .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The situation is significantly more involved in the case $d=3$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Here the flow occurs in a multi-dimensional parameter space and even finding all its fixed points presents a non-trivial task.
elements.sent-2411-028: A first step towards elucidating the global properties of the RG flow of the three-dimensional projectable HG was made in Ref.~\cite{Barvinsky:2023uir .}
elements.sent-2411-029: It found a family of RG trajectories flowing from one of the asymptotically free fixed points in the UV towards an infrared (IR) region in the parameter space where the kinetic term in the Lagrangian of the theory approaches the same form as in GR.
elements.sent-2411-030: This IR limit was assumed in most phenomenological applications \cite{Mukohyama:2009mz,Izumi:2009ry,Izumi:2011eh,Gumrukcuoglu:2011ef, Bassani:2024lan and corresponds to the regime where projectable HG behaves (at least at the classical level) similarly to GR supplemented with a dust-like matter.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the present work we complete the study of the RG flow in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-032: We focus on the marginal operators with respect to the scaling (\ref{scaling ) since they form a closed set under renormalization and dominate the flow in an infinite span of energies above the Lorentz violating scale.}
elements.sent-2411-033: First, we numerically find all fixed points of the flow and classify their properties.
elements.sent-2411-034: Second, we characterize the local properies of the flow around the fixed points by computing the repsective stability matrices.
elements.sent-2411-035: Third, we study the global structure of the RG trajectories emanating from asymptoically free fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-036: We find that, apart from the family found in \cite{Barvinsky:2023uir , all trajectories quickly run into strong coupling, without reaching any GR-like regime.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In other words, the family of Ref.~\cite{Barvinsky:2023uir exhausts all RG trajectories connecting consistent UV asymptotics with phenomenologically interesting IR behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-038: It is worth noting that the RG flow reaching a proper IR domain is necessary but not sufficient condition for phenomenological viability.
elements.sent-2411-039: Indeed, the full IR limit of projectable HG including the relevant operators with respect to the scaling (\ref{scaling ) features an instability which can be suppressed only at the expense of introducing strong coupling \cite{Koyama:2009hc,Blas:2010hb .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In this paper we do not attempt to address this issue and consider the projectable HG as a toy model for possible RG structure of more complicated versions of the theory with stable IR dynamics, such as e.g. the non-projectable model.
elements.sent-2411-041: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-042: In Sec.~\ref{sec:projectableHG we review the action of the projectable HG and its one-loop beta-functions.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Sec.~\ref{sec:FPs we describe our algorithm for the numerical search of fixed points and give their complete list.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section~\ref{SectStabMat analyzes the stability matrices of the fixed points.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In Sec.~\ref{SectRGflows we turn to the global properties of the RG flow and study the trajectories emanating from asymptotically free fixed points.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Scanning over their possible initial conditions, we identify the unique family of trajectories which exhibits interesting IR behavior.
elements.sent-2411-047: Section~\ref{sec:strength further explores the RG running of the gravitational coupling along the family and points out a natural hierarchy arising between the Planck mass and the scale of Lorentz violation, the former being much larger than the latter.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Some technical details are relegated to Appendices.
elements.sent-2411-050: HG is defined using the Arnowitt--Deser--Misner (ADM) decomposition of the metric \be \di s^2 = N^2 \di t^2 - \gamma_{ij (\di x^i + N^i \di t) (\di x^j + N^j \di t)\,, \ee where $N$ is the lapse function, $N^i$ is the shift vector and $\g_{ij}$ is the spatial metric.}
elements.sent-2411-051: These fields are assigned the following scaling dimensions with respect to \eqref{scaling :\footnote{One says that a field $\Phi$ has scaling dimension $[\Phi]$ if under (\ref{scaling ) it transforms as $\Phi\mapsto b^{[\Phi]}\Phi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The potential part ${\cal V}$ does not contain any time derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-053: It depends on the $d$-dimensional metric $\g_{ij}$ and its spatial derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-054: In what follows we specify to $d=3$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Note that in the case of GR written in the ADM form the four-dimensional diffeomorphism invariance forces the parameter $\l$ in the kinetic term for the metric to be equal to one, $\l=1$.
elements.sent-2411-056: In the case of non-projectable model where the lapse is a full-fledged function of time and space, ${\cal V}$ also depends on the acceleration vector $a_i = \p_i N/N$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The low-energy limit of the theory corresponds to a scalar-tensor gravity, with the scalar describing a preferred foliation of spacetime by space-like surfaces \cite{Germani:2009yt,Blas:2009yd,Kimpton:2010xi,Blas:2010hb .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The dynamics of the scalar is stable and weakly coupled, its interaction with visible matter and gravity can be suppressed by an appropriate choice of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-059: This includes, in particular, a constraint $0<\l-1\ll1$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, the model can reproduce the phenomenology of GR at the scales where the latter has been tested \cite{Blas:2014aca .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The strongest constraints on the model come from tests of the Lorentz invariance in the matter sector \cite{Kostelecky:2008ts,Liberati:2013xla and gravity \cite{Monitor:2017mdv , but still leave a viable parameter space \cite{EmirGumrukcuoglu:2017cfa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: In the non-projectable model the total number of terms in the potential is very large (order $O(100)$) \cite{Kimpton:2013zb .\footnote{Most of these terms are irrelevant at low energies and get important only in the UV.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This complicates the analysis of the UV properties of the theory.
elements.sent-2411-064: Another complication comes from the fact that the propagator of the lapse contains certain irregular terms that can potentially give rise to divergences with nonlocal space dependence and thus spoil renormalizability \cite{Barvinsky:2015kil .}
elements.sent-2411-065: An important step towards overcoming the latter problem was made in \cite{Bellorin:2022np which showed that the dangerous divergences cancel if one defines the path integral of the theory using a proper integration measure.}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, the results of \cite{Bellorin:2022np are obtained within the canonical approach which is inconvenient for carrying out renormalization.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Their reformulation in the Lagrangian language is pending.

elements.section-2411-003: Fixed points of the RG flow
elements.sent-2411-101: We start the investigation of the RG flow by searching for its fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-102: Of particular interest are the asymptotically free fixed points corresponding to vanishing gravitational coupling ${\cal G}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The coupling $\l$ at a fixed point can be either finite or infinite.
elements.sent-2411-104: The latter case $\l=\infty$ was shown to represent a regular weakly coupled limit of the theory \cite{Gumrukcuoglu:2011xg,Radkovski:2023cew .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The only restriction we impose on the values of $\l$ is that they lie inside the unitary domain (\ref{lambdaunitary ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: The other couplings $u_s$, $v_a$ are required to take finite values at the fixed point.
elements.sent-2411-107: Beta-functions \eqref{betafun are defined as derivatives of the couplings with respect to $\log k_\star$, the logarithm of the sliding momentum scale.\footnote{Note that the logarithm of momentum is different from the logarithm of energy by a factor $1/3$ due to the Lifshitz scaling, $d\log k_\star=\tfrac{1 {3 d\log E_\star$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: It is convenient to change the parameterization of the RG trajectories by introducing new independent variable $\tau$ through \be d\tau = {\cal G}\, d\log k_\star\;, \ee and redefine the beta-functions, \be \frac{dg_i {d\tau =\tilde \beta_{g_i ,\qquad g_i=\{\l,u_s,v_1,v_2,v_3\}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Thus, the search for fixed points reduce to finding the roots of the system of equations \be \tilde\beta_{g_i = 0\; \ee for the five variables $g_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The functions $\tilde\beta_{g_i}$ are, up to some non-vanishing factors, polynomial in the couplings $g_i$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Thus, in principle, all roots of the system (\ref{fullsystem ) can be found with the Buchberger algorithm \cite{Buchberger:2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-112: In practice, however, this is unfeasible.
elements.sent-2411-113: Indeed, according to the B\'ezout's theorem \cite{Shafarevich the number of (complex) roots of a system of polynomial equations is in general equal to the product of the degrees of the polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The degree of the polynomial in $\tilde\beta_\l$ is $4$, whereas an inspection of the expressions for ${\cal P _n^\chi$ given in \cite{3+1 yields the degrees of $\tilde\beta_{u_s}$ and $\tilde\beta_{v_a}$, $a=1,2,3$, to be $15$ and $17$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: This gives $4 \cdot 15 \cdot 17^3= 294\,780$ as the possible number of roots.
elements.sent-2411-116: While the actual number of roots can be lower due to the special structure of the polynomials in $\tilde\beta_{g_i}$, it is still likely to be forbiddingly large.
elements.sent-2411-117: Fortunately, we need only {\it real roots of the system (\ref{fullsystem ).}}
elements.sent-2411-118: These can be found by means of a scanning algorithm which we presently describe.
elements.sent-2411-119: We separately discuss the cases of finite and infinite $\l$.
elements.sent-2411-120: We note that $\tilde\beta_\lambda$ depends only on $u_s$ and $\l$.
elements.sent-2411-121: The solution of the equation $\tilde\beta_\lambda = 0$ relates these variables\footnote{We choose the positive value of the square root in (\ref{lamus ).}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The opposite choice would result in $\l$ lying in the non-unitary region $[1/3, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Similarly, the positive square root in (\ref{uslam ) ensures that $u_s$ is positive.}

elements.sent-2411-374: We performed a comprehensive study of the RG flow of marginal couplings in $(3+1)$-dimensional projectable HG.
elements.sent-2411-375: We first identified all fixed points of the flow: five points at finite value of the kinetic coupling $\l$ and eight points at $\l=\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-376: We presented strong numerical evidence that no other fixed points exist.
elements.sent-2411-377: All fixed points at finite $\l$ are asymptotically free.
elements.sent-2411-378: However, unlike the case of HG in $(2+1)$-dimensions \cite{Barvinsky:2017kob , they all lie at $\l<1/3$, in the left part of the domain (\ref{lambdaunitary ) allowed by unitarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-379: Thus, they cannot be connected by RG trajectories to the phenomenologically interesting region $\l\to1^+$.
elements.sent-2411-380: Three fixed points out of five are very close to the boundary of the unitary domain $\l=1/3$ and feature very large values of couplings $\{u_s,v_1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-381: Out of the eight fixed points at infinity only three points are asymptotically free and can give rise to trajectories flowing towards $\l\to1^+$.
elements.sent-2411-382: We next analyzed the local properties of the RG flow around the fixed points by studying eigenvalues and eigenvectors of their stability matrices.
elements.sent-2411-383: This allowed us to identify the attractive and repulsive directions of the fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-384: Quite unexpectedly, we found that some fixed points are characterized by complex conjugate pairs of eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-385: This appears at clash with the intuition that the eigenvalues of the stability matrix are associated with the anomalous dimensions of the operators generating the RG flow.
elements.sent-2411-386: While complex anomalous dimensions are quite common in non-unitary theories (see e.g. \cite{Hogervorst:2015akt,Gorbenko:2018ncu,Gromov:2017cja,Jepsen:2020czw ), they are believed to be incompatible with unitarity.}
elements.sent-2411-387: Still, we do not think that the presence of complex eigenvalues of the stability matrix signals violation of unitarity in HG.
elements.sent-2411-388: Rather, the relation between properties of the fixed points and unitarity can be subtle because HG is a gauge theory.
elements.sent-2411-389: In non-Abelian theories the gauge group degenerates into its linearized version at asymptotically free fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-390: The operators describing interactions and generating the RG flow are not invariant under this linearized gauge group.
elements.sent-2411-391: In other words, they do not belong to the spectrum of gauge invariant operators at the (free) fixed point and unitarity constraints do not apply to them.
elements.sent-2411-392: Besides, it should be reminded that in the case of HG the dimensions of operators are defined with respect to the anisotropic (Lifshitz) scaling.
elements.sent-2411-393: We are not aware of any rigorous reality conditions in general Lifshitz theories.
elements.sent-2411-394: Exploring unitarity constraints on the dimensions of operators and RG flows in such theories will be an interesting task.
elements.sent-2411-395: To the best of our knowledge, HG is the first example of a unitary theory with complex eigenvalues of the stability matrix.
elements.sent-2411-396: It would be interesting to search for more theories with this property.
elements.sent-2411-397: With the insight from the local analysis we moved to the global properties of RG trajectories emanating from asymptotically free fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-398: We considered two fixed points at finite $\l$ and moderate $\{u_s,v_1\}$, initiating a family of trajectories along their repulsive directions.
elements.sent-2411-399: We found that all such trajectories run into singularity, with some couplings diverging in a finite `RG time'.
elements.sent-2411-400: Similar behavior is exhibited by trajectories starting from two of the three fixed points at $\l=\infty$ which we previously identified as potentially interesting.
elements.sent-2411-401: The parameter $\l$ along these trajectories remains very large all the way from the fixed point to the singularity.
elements.sent-2411-402: In particular, the trajectories from these points never reach into the phenomenologically interesting region $\l\to1^+$.
elements.sent-2411-403: However, one fixed point at $\l=\infty$, which we called point A, does give rise to long trajectories interpolating to $\l\to1^+$ or $\l\to1/3^-$, depending on the initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-404: The RG flow emanating from the point A thus covers the whole range (\ref{lambdaunitary ) allowed by unitarity.}
elements.sent-2411-405: The RG trajectories have a peculiar structure: upon leaving the point A they get attracted to another, not asymptotically free, fixed point B also at $\l=\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-406: Coming close to the point B, they `scatter' on it and continue along its single repulsive direction towards $\l=O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-407: The trajectories from a family with one of the two asymptotics ($\l\to1^+$ or $\l\to1/3^-$) are very close to each other in the space of couplings $\{\l,u_s,v_1,v_2,v_3\}$.
elements.sent-2411-408: What distinguishes them is the running of the overall gravitational coupling~${\cal G}$.
elements.sent-2411-409: Remarkably, this running is not monotonic, with ${\cal G}$ becoming very small both in UV and IR.
elements.sent-2411-410: We argued that, despite vanishing of ${\cal G}$, the theory becomes strongly coupled in IR if the RG flow gets too close to the boundary of the unitary domain $\l=1$.
elements.sent-2411-411: This running into strong coupling can be prevented by the relevant operators which will cut the flow at some finite IR values $\l_{IR}$ and ${\cal G _{IR}$.}
elements.sent-2411-412: We showed that if $\l_{IR}$ is order-one, the value ${\cal G _{IR}$ is naturally tiny.}
elements.sent-2411-413: This implies that the Planck mass inferred from the strength of gravitational interactions in the low-energy theory is hierarchically larger --- by as many as 10 orders of magnitude --- than the scale of Lorentz symmetry breaking.
elements.sent-2411-414: If such natural strong hierarchy arises also in the non-projectable HG, it can strongly suppress its deviations from GR at low energies \cite{Blas:2010hb,Blas:2014aca,EmirGumrukcuoglu:2017cfa , as well as leakage of Lorentz violating effects from gravity to the matter sector \cite{Pospelov:2010mp .}}
elements.sent-2411-415: The study of this intriguing possibility is left for future.
